lead: Moving day can feel like a great unknown, especially if you have never used a professional removals team before. What actually happens? When does the crew arrive, how does the day unfold, and what is your part in it all? After two decades of moving Suffolk families, we know that knowing what to expect takes a great deal of the worry away. So here is an honest, hour-by-hour sense of how a well-run moving day really goes, and how to make yours as smooth as it can be.

sections.h: The Morning: Arrival and First Steps
sections.body:
A well-organised moving day starts early, and the crew will usually arrive at an agreed time in the morning with the right vehicle and the right number of people for your move. The first thing a good team does is not start grabbing boxes; it is to take a moment with you to confirm the plan. We will walk through the house, check what is going and what is staying, note anything fragile or special, and agree how the day will flow.

This short conversation matters more than it looks. It is your chance to point out the items that need particular care, the boxes that must travel with you rather than on the lorry, and anything you have not had a chance to mention before. It also lets the crew confirm access, parking and the route out, so everyone is working from the same picture. A move that begins with a clear, shared plan almost always runs more smoothly than one that begins in a rush.

Once the plan is agreed, the work begins in earnest. The crew will start protecting the home, laying coverings where needed and padding banisters and doorways, before methodically loading the lorry. Your job at this stage is mostly to stay out of the way of the carry, keep children and pets safe and settled, and be on hand for the occasional question. The hardest physical work is ours; yours is to keep the day calm and answer the odd query.

sections.h: Loading: How a Good Crew Works
sections.body:
Loading a lorry well is a craft, not a scramble, and watching an experienced crew do it is genuinely reassuring. There is a logic to the order: heavy and sturdy items form a stable base, furniture is padded and secured, fragile boxes are kept clear of crushing weight, and everything is packed so it cannot shift on the road. A good crew is thinking several steps ahead about every journey the van will make, not just filling space.

You will notice the team works steadily and methodically rather than frantically. That measured pace is deliberate, because rushing is how damage happens, both to belongings and to homes. Items are carried carefully through protected doorways, awkward pieces are handled by enough hands to do it safely, and anything that needs dismantling, such as beds or large wardrobes, is taken apart with the fittings kept safe for reassembly at the other end.

This is also when the labelling you did during packing earns its keep. Clearly marked boxes let the crew load with the unloading in mind and tell them at a glance what needs careful handling. If you have an essentials box for the first night, this is the moment to make sure it stays with you rather than going on the lorry. A little organisation on your part lets the crew work faster and smarter, which benefits everyone.

sections.h: Between Homes: The Journey and Any Storage
sections.body:
Once the lorry is loaded, there is the journey to the new home, and what that looks like depends on your move. For a local Suffolk move it may be a short hop; for a longer move it could be a good drive, occasionally with the realistic timings of country lanes or main roads factored in. The crew handles all of this, and you are free to travel separately, deal with handovers, or simply get yourself to the new place ahead of the van.

Sometimes the new home is not ready the moment the old one is emptied, perhaps because of a gap in the chain or a completion that slips. This is where storage comes in, and a good firm will have planned for it rather than being caught out. Your belongings can be held securely until the new home is ready, then delivered when you are, so a hitch in timing does not become a crisis. If storage is part of your move, it should already be arranged and explained well before the day.

sections.h: The New Home: Unloading and Placement
sections.body:
At the new home, the process runs in reverse, and again it begins with a quick plan. The crew will ask where you want things to go, which is exactly why labelling boxes by room pays off so handsomely. Rather than dumping everything in the hallway, a good team carries boxes and furniture straight to the right rooms, reassembles the beds and wardrobes they dismantled, and positions the heavy items where you want them while there are still hands to do it.

Take advantage of this stage, because moving a wardrobe or a sofa later, alone, is far harder than having the crew place it correctly now. Walk through and check that furniture is where you want it before the team finishes, and point out anything that needs adjusting. It is much easier to get it right while the crew is there than to wrestle with it yourself the following week, so do not be shy about directing where things should land.

Before the crew leaves, there will be a final check together. We will make sure the lorry is empty, that nothing has been left behind, that the items you flagged have arrived safely, and that you are happy with where everything has been placed. This is also the moment to raise anything at all, because a quick word now is always better than a worry later. A good move ends with a calm handover, not a hurried departure.

sections.h: What's Expected of You on the Day
sections.body:
Your role on moving day is lighter than you might fear, but a few things genuinely help. Be ready when the crew arrives, with packing finished and boxes labelled, so the day can start without delay. Keep the essentials box, your valuables and important documents with you personally rather than on the lorry. Make sure pets and small children are settled somewhere safe and out of the path of the carry, ideally with someone keeping an eye on them.

Beyond that, stay reachable and decisive. The crew may have the occasional question, and a quick answer keeps things moving. Have meter readings noted, keys and access sorted at both ends, and the kettle box handy so everyone can have a well-earned cup of tea. You do not need to lift a thing if you would rather not; your job is to keep the day organised and let the experienced team carry the heavy and technical work.

sections.h: A Calm, Well-Run Day Is the Norm
sections.body:
The biggest thing to expect from a properly handled moving day is that it is far calmer than the dread that precedes it. When the planning has been done, the quote was accurate, and the crew is experienced and settled, the day flows in a way that surprises people. The chaos that haunts moving-day nightmares almost always comes from poor preparation, not from the move itself, which is precisely why we put so much weight on getting the groundwork right.

So expect an early start, a clear plan, a careful crew, and a calm handover at the end, all fully covered from the first box to the last. If you would like that kind of moving day, the sort you look back on with relief rather than horror, we would be glad to help. Get in touch for a free, no-obligation quote, and we will talk you through exactly how your day will run so there are no surprises when the morning comes.

faqs: What time does the crew arrive on moving day?

A well-organised move usually starts early, with the crew arriving at an agreed morning time. The first step is a quick walk-through with you to confirm the plan, check access and note anything fragile or special, before the protecting and loading begins in earnest.
Do I need to help carry things on the day?

Not unless you want to. The heavy and technical work is ours. Your role is to be ready when we arrive, keep the essentials box and valuables with you, keep pets and children safe, and be on hand to answer the occasional question and direct where things go.
What happens if my new home isn't ready in time?

If there is a gap, your belongings can be held securely in storage until the new home is ready, then delivered when you are. A good firm plans for this in advance, so a slipped completion or a chain hiccup does not turn into a crisis on the day.
How does the crew know where to put everything?

By asking you and by reading your box labels, which is why labelling each box with its room pays off. We carry boxes and furniture straight to the right rooms and reassemble beds and wardrobes, so check placement before we finish while there are still hands to adjust things.
